mappings.rationaleAI: Linnaeus created binomial nomenclature and the hierarchical taxonomy of kingdom, class, order, genus, and species that remains the foundational framework for classifying all living things across every category in this section.

Aristotle originated systematic biological classification through comparative observation of diverse life forms, establishing methods for categorizing organisms that preceded and influenced Linnaean taxonomy.

Slime moulds are the organisms classified at C.3 in this outline, and the episode directly examines their resistance to standard taxonomic placement, with species spanning multiple kingdoms and defying plant-animal-fungus boundaries.
Carl Woese's identification of archaea as a domain entirely separate from bacteria fundamentally restructured the classification of prokaryotic life, directly relevant to the monerans category at B.

Substantive treatment of fungi as a distinct taxonomic kingdom separate from plants and animals, covering their biology, enormous species diversity, reproduction, and ecological roles that define the group at D.

Detailed examination of cephalopods — octopus, squid, nautilus, and cuttlefish — as a class of molluscs, covering their distinguishing biological features including intelligence, colour change, and jet propulsion.
Corals are cnidarian anthozoans classified at F.4.a; the episode examines their biology as simple colonial animals and reef ecology, directly relevant to this taxonomic group.
Bacteriophages are the most abundant viruses on Earth; the episode covers their biology, discovery, and diversity as a major category within the viral classification at A.
Surveys the major groups of microorganisms — bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, and archaea — spanning multiple taxonomic categories including viruses, monerans, protozoans, and fungi.

Covers parasitic organisms across multiple animal phyla including flatworms (F.6), nematodes (F.9.d), and parasitic insects (F.14.b), illustrating the taxonomic breadth of parasitism within the animal kingdom.
Focuses on angiosperm reproductive biology and co-evolutionary relationships shaping flower morphology, directly relevant to the classification of flowering plants at E.10.
Covers phytoplankton as photosynthetic algae (C.1) and zooplankton as diverse microscopic organisms (C.2), illustrating the taxonomic range within protist categories.
Covers photosynthesis in cyanobacteria (prokaryotic monerans at B), algae (protist algae at C.1), and green plants (E), linking these taxonomic groups through their shared biochemical capability.

Examines mammalian diversification as a major chordate group, covering defining characteristics — warm-bloodedness, lactation, fur, live birth — that distinguish mammals taxonomically within chordates at F.25.

Traces crocodilian evolutionary history revealing far greater ancestral morphological diversity than modern species suggest, relevant to understanding reptilian classification within chordates at F.25.

Wallace founded biogeography as a science and contributed to understanding geographic distribution patterns of species, establishing principles that inform the classification and evolutionary relationships of plants and animals.

Traces respiratory evolution across vertebrate chordates from fish through amphibians to terrestrial forms, using comparative anatomy that illuminates the classification of major chordate subgroups at F.25.
